title: Natural Selection
definition: The differential survival and reproduction of individuals due to differences in heritable traits. Over generations, traits that enhance survival and reproduction become more common in the population, driving adaptive evolution.

proofs.description: Observed evolution in real time: antibiotic resistance, Darwin's finches beak size, peppered moths
proofs.reference: Weiner, J. (1994). The Beak of the Finch.

proofs.description: Population genetics formalizes natural selection mathematically
proofs.reference: Fisher, R.A. (1930). The Genetical Theory of Natural Selection.

difficulty_levels.beginner: Living things with traits that help them survive and reproduce tend to pass those traits to their offspring. Over time, the population becomes better adapted to its environment.
difficulty_levels.intermediate: Natural selection is the differential reproductive success of heritable variants. It requires variation, heritability, and differential fitness. It explains adaptation without a designer.
difficulty_levels.expert: Natural selection operates on phenotypes but acts on genotypes via inclusive fitness. The modern synthesis integrates selection with population genetics. Selection can be stabilizing, directional, or disruptive. Selection acts at multiple levels: gene, individual, kin, group.

extra_fields.historical_context: Independently proposed by Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace (1858). Darwin's On the Origin of Species (1859) provided extensive evidence. Modern synthesis (1930s-40s) integrated with genetics.
extra_fields.limitations: Does not explain origin of life (abiogenesis), cultural evolution, or traits with no fitness differential. Neutral theory (Kimura) explains much molecular evolution.
